county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Delaware County formed 1907 at statehood from the Cherokee Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Jay. Named for the Delaware (Lenape) tribe. Located in the Ozark region of northeastern Oklahoma.
county_metadata.research_tips: Delaware County was part of the Cherokee Nation in Indian Territory. The county is named for the Delaware (Lenape) tribe that settled in the area after removal from the eastern United States. Both Cherokee Nation and Delaware tribal records are relevant. Dawes Rolls include Cherokee Delaware enrollees. NARA Fort Worth holds Bureau of Indian Affairs records for the Union Agency. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory court records. County Clerk records in Jay begin at statehood (1907). The county has significant Ozark heritage with early Arkansan and Missourian settlers. Grove and Jay public libraries have local genealogical collections. The Delaware County Historical Society has pioneer family records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Delaware County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.record_losses: Courthouse fire in 1916 destroyed most early records including deeds, probate, and marriage records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Dewey County formed 1892 from former Cheyenne and Arapaho lands. County seat is Taloga. Named for Admiral George Dewey. Courthouse fire 1916 destroyed most early records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Dewey County was organized from former Cheyenne and Arapaho lands (1892). A devastating courthouse fire in 1916 destroyed most early records including deeds, probate records, and marriage records. For records before 1916, researchers must rely on alternative sources: BLM GLO land patents at glorecords.blm.gov, NARA Fort Worth Cheyenne-Arapaho Agency records, Oklahoma Historical Society territorial newspapers and census records, and church records. Post-fire County Clerk records in Taloga begin from 1916. The fire makes Dewey County one of Oklahoma's most challenging counties for early genealogical research. FamilySearch has some Oklahoma marriage records. The Dewey County Historical Society has attempted to reconstruct some lost records from alternative sources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Dewey County; County Clerk deed books from 1892

county_metadata.parent_county: Day County / Roger Mills County, Oklahoma Territory
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Ellis County formed 1907 at statehood from Day County and part of Roger Mills County. County seat is Arnett. Named for Albert H. Ellis. Day County was an extinct territorial county absorbed at statehood.
county_metadata.research_tips: Ellis County was created at statehood (1907) from Day County and part of Roger Mills County. For pre-1907 records, check Roger Mills County and the extinct Day County (absorbed into Ellis, Dewey, and Roger Mills at statehood). County Clerk records in Arnett begin from 1907. BLM GLO land patents are available online. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers and court records. The area was settled after the Cheyenne-Arapaho opening (1892). Ranching and farming dominated the economy. The Ellis County Historical Society has local family records and cemetery transcriptions. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original county, Oklahoma Territory (Cherokee Outlet run)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Garfield County formed 1893 from the Cherokee Outlet (land run). County seat is Enid. Named for James A. Garfield. Major agricultural and railroad center.
county_metadata.research_tips: Garfield County was formed from the Cherokee Outlet, opened by land run on September 16, 1893. Enid, the county seat, became a major railroad and agricultural center. County Clerk records begin from 1893. BLM GLO land patents document original Cherokee Outlet claims. The Enid and Garfield County records are among the more complete territorial-era records in western Oklahoma. The Cherokee Strip Regional Heritage Center in Enid is an excellent local repository with pioneer family records, photographs, and oral histories. The Enid Public Library has city directories, newspaper indexes, and local history collections. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers (the Enid Eagle and others). The Garfield County Genealogical Society has published cemetery transcriptions and family histories. FamilySearch has Oklahoma territorial and state marriage records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Garfield County; County Clerk deed books from 1893

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Garvin County formed 1907 at statehood from the Chickasaw Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Pauls Valley. Named for Samuel Garvin, a Chickasaw leader.
county_metadata.research_tips: Garvin County was part of the Chickasaw Nation in Indian Territory. The county seat, Pauls Valley, is named for Smith Paul, a Chickasaw citizen and entrepreneur. Chickasaw Nation tribal records (Dawes Rolls, allotment records, citizenship files) are at the Chickasaw Nation headquarters in Ada and NARA Fort Worth. The Chickasaw Cultural Center near Sulphur has historical materials. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). Oil production in the county generated lease and royalty records. The Pauls Valley Public Library and Garvin County Historical Society have local genealogical collections. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory court records and newspapers. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Garvin County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.parent_county: Chickasaw Nation / Caddo County, Indian/Oklahoma Territory
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Grady County formed 1907 at statehood from portions of Chickasaw Nation and Caddo County. County seat is Chickasha. Named for Henry W. Grady. Boundary county between Twin Territories.
county_metadata.research_tips: Grady County was formed at statehood (1907) from portions of both Chickasaw Nation (Indian Territory) and Caddo County (Oklahoma Territory), making it a unique boundary county between the Twin Territories. For Chickasaw Nation heritage, check Dawes Rolls and allotment records at NARA Fort Worth. For Oklahoma Territory heritage, check BLM GLO land patents. County Clerk records in Chickasha begin from 1907. Chickasha was a significant railroad town. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds newspapers and court records. The Grady County Historical Society and Chickasha Public Library have local genealogical collections. Oil production records (leases, royalties) are at the County Clerk. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original county, Oklahoma Territory (Cherokee Outlet run)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Grant County formed 1893 from the Cherokee Outlet (land run). County seat is Medford. Named for Ulysses S. Grant. Agricultural county in north-central Oklahoma.
county_metadata.research_tips: Grant County was formed from the Cherokee Outlet, opened by land run on September 16, 1893. County Clerk records in Medford begin from 1893. BLM GLO land patents document original Cherokee Outlet claims. The county was predominantly agricultural with wheat farming. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers and census records. German-born settlers and their descendants established Lutheran and Catholic congregations -- church records may be valuable. The Grant County Museum in Medford has pioneer family records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma territorial and state marriage records. The Grant County Genealogical Society has published cemetery transcriptions.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Grant County; County Clerk deed books from 1893

county_metadata.parent_county: Annexed from Texas (Greer County dispute settled by US Supreme Court)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Greer County formed 1896 after US Supreme Court ruling (United States v. Texas) transferred the area from Texas to Oklahoma Territory. County seat is Mangum. Unique dual-state jurisdictional history (Texas 1860-1896, Oklahoma 1896-present).
county_metadata.research_tips: Greer County has a unique jurisdictional history: it was claimed by Texas until the US Supreme Court ruled it part of Oklahoma Territory in 1896 (United States v. Texas). Researchers must check Texas records for the period 1860-1896 (Greer County was organized as a Texas county in 1886). Texas vital records, land records, and census records may contain Greer County entries. After 1896, records are in Oklahoma Territory. County Clerk records in Mangum include both Texas-era and Oklahoma-era records. BLM GLO land patents are available online. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers. The Greer County Heritage Museum in Mangum has local materials. FamilySearch has both Texas and Oklahoma records for this area. This dual-state heritage makes Greer County research uniquely complex.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Greer County; County Clerk deed books from 1896

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Harmon County formed 1909 from Greer County. County seat is Hollis. Named for Judson Harmon. Inherits Greer County's dual-state jurisdictional history (formerly Texas).
county_metadata.research_tips: Harmon County was created in 1909 from Greer County, inheriting Greer's unique jurisdictional history (formerly claimed by Texas until 1896). For pre-1909 records, check Greer County and Texas records. County Clerk records in Hollis begin from 1909. BLM GLO land patents are available online. The county was primarily agricultural (cotton and wheat). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds newspapers and territorial records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage records. Cemetery transcriptions are available through Find A Grave. The Harmon County Historical Society has local family materials.

source_profiles.source_name: Harmon County Marriage Records -- County Clerk / OHS / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Harmon County Clerk, Hollis / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and records for Harmon County from 1909; County Clerk issued marriage licenses in Oklahoma; OHS holds some county marriage records on microfilm; FamilySearch has indexed Oklahoma marriages; state vital records registration began 1908

source_profiles.source_name: Harmon County Cemetery Records -- Find A Grave / OKGenWeb
source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / OKGenWeb / Harmon County Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions for Harmon County including church cemeteries, municipal cemeteries, and pioneer burial grounds; Find A Grave has growing coverage; OKGenWeb and local historical societies have published transcriptions

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Harper County formed 1907 at statehood from Woodward County and Cherokee Outlet. County seat is Buffalo. Named for Oscar G. Harper.
county_metadata.research_tips: Harper County was created at statehood (1907) from Woodward County and the Cherokee Outlet. For pre-1907 records, check Woodward County. County Clerk records in Buffalo begin from 1907. BLM GLO land patents document original Cherokee Outlet claims (1893 run). The county was predominantly agricultural and ranching. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers and census records. The Harper County Historical Society and Buffalo Public Library have local genealogical collections. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records. Kansas border-area families may have records in both states.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Harper County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

source_profiles.source_name: Harper County Deed Books / BLM GLO Patents -- County Clerk
source_profiles.repository: Harper County Clerk, Buffalo / Bureau of Land Management
source_profiles.coverage_notes: County deed books for Harper County from 1907; BLM GLO federal land patents document original claims from the 1893 Cherokee Outlet run; County Clerk maintains deeds, mortgages, and liens
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1907; GLO patents are complete for federal land entries

source_profiles.source_name: Harper County Probate Records -- District Court
source_profiles.repository: Harper County District Court, Buffalo
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories, guardianships, and estate settlements for Harper County; District Court has jurisdiction over probate in Oklahoma; guardianship records for minor allottees are particularly important in former Indian Territory counties

source_profiles.source_name: Harper County Federal and Tribal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1910-1950 for Harper County; Oklahoma Territory schedules; OHS holds territorial census records

source_profiles.source_name: Harper County Land Run / Homestead Records -- BLM GLO / NARA / OHS
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management / NARA Fort Worth / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents (homestead, cash entry) for Harper County from the 1893 Cherokee Outlet run; BLM GLO website has digitized patents; NARA Fort Worth holds Oklahoma Territory land office records including homestead applications and final proof files with affidavits and witness testimony; OHS has territorial census and newspaper records
